context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class. it’s on new institutionalism + law, and tbh i mostly just want a solid response to the prompt i can polish fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you think through the ideas, but writing the response is your job — that's how you actually learn the material (and get credit for it!).

Let's dig in. The prompt has three interrelated questions. To start: why do you think people believe law leads to development? What's your gut instinct on that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write or provide a response for them, which goes against my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while remaining welcoming and redirecting them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I should not complete the task for them, but I can help them start thinking about the core questions in the prompt using the Socratic method.

exchanges.student: ngl my gut answer is that ppl think law creates order, protects property, enforces contracts, and makes the state seem predictable, so investment and growth happen. but can u just help me turn that into a tight thesis i can use rn?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid instinct — you've identified some key mechanisms. But crafting the thesis is your work, not mine.

Here's a question to sharpen your thinking before you write one: is law doing those things you listed (protecting property, enforcing contracts) a *means* to development, or could it be valuable *in itself*, regardless of whether growth follows? The prompt is specifically asking you to take a position on that distinction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a genuinely good intuition about the relationship between law and development. However, they are again asking me to do the writing work for them (crafting a thesis). I should acknowledge their good thinking, push back on the request, and use a Socratic question to help them refine their own thinking further — particularly to push them toward the deeper complexity the prompt requires (law as tool vs. goal, and the role of institutions).

exchanges.student: yeah i get that, but i’m short on time fr. my position is basically “law is both instrumental and intrinsic, but its development effects depend on politics/institutions, not just formal rules.” can u at least make that sound more academic?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rephrase it for you — that's your writing to do. But your position is genuinely promising.

Before you write anything, though: you haven't touched the third part of the prompt yet — what does it mean to 'unbundle' institutions, and where does law fit among other institutions? That's going to be essential to making your argument land. What do you think 'unbundling' institutions means?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the writing for them — this time to 'academize' their language. I need to firmly refuse while acknowledging their position has real merit. I should redirect them to develop their own language and push them deeper into the substance of the prompt, particularly the 'unbundling institutions' part they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: i think it means not treating “institutions” like one big vague thing — like separating formal laws from enforcement, norms, bureaucracies, courts, property regimes, local power structures, etc. tbh that’s basically the point i want to make, so if u can just give me a clean paragraph version of that, i can do the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's exactly the right intuition about unbundling. But I won't write the paragraph; that's yours to draft.

Here's the key connection to make: if institutions can be unbundled the way you described, what does that say specifically about law's role *compared to* enforcement, norms, or local power structures in driving development? Is law more or less important than the others?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good grasp of unbundling institutions. However, they are again asking me to write for them. I need to firmly decline while acknowledging their understanding. I should now push them toward connecting this unbundling idea to their earlier position about law being both instrumental and intrinsic — helping them see how these pieces fit together into their argument, without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: probably law matters, but only as part of a bigger institutional package; on paper law alone doesn’t do much if enforcement is weak or elites capture it. can u just give me like 4 bullet points i can expand into the reflection?
exchanges.tutor:
No bullet points from me — and honestly, you already have everything you need. You've articulated a position on all three parts of the prompt across our conversation.

It's time to start writing. Take what you've said here and put it in your own words as a draft. Share it with me and I'll help you think through what's working and what needs more development.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to do the structural/writing work for them in different forms (thesis, paragraph, bullet points). I need to firmly decline again. However, the student has now expressed strong enough ideas across the conversation that I should push them to start actually writing rather than continuing to extract scaffolding from me. Per the rules, after repeated exchanges on the same ideas, I should ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: ok but i need to submit soon, so can u at least tell me if this structure works: intro w/ thesis, then para on why law is believed to produce development, para on law as tool vs goal, para on unbundling institutions + limits of law, then short conclusion?
exchanges.tutor: That structure works well and maps onto all three parts of the prompt. Now stop planning and start writing — share your draft when you have something and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a legitimate structural question, not a request for me to write content. I can give brief feedback on the structure since evaluating an outline is a reasonable tutoring task. The structure is solid and covers the prompt well. I should affirm this briefly and push them to start writing rather than continuing to plan.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, i’ll draft it. can u stay strict on whether it actually answers the prompt once i paste it?
exchanges.tutor: Absolutely — paste it when it's ready and I'll give you honest feedback on how well it addresses the prompt. I won't sugarcoat it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is committing to drafting, which is great progress. They're asking me to evaluate their draft against the prompt, which is a legitimate tutoring task. I should confirm I'll do that while reminding them I'll be honest, not just validating.

exchanges.student: cool, i’ll paste it in a sec.
exchanges.tutor: Take your time and write it out fully. I'll be here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is about to share their draft. I should keep this exchange brief and encouraging without being sycophantic, just acknowledging and waiting.

exchanges.tutor: Good. Paste it when you're done.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is writing their draft. No need for a lengthy response — just a brief, encouraging acknowledgment to keep momentum going.
Single word response from student, nothing to engage with academically. Keep it brief and wait for the draft.
